title: A Lover's Litanies - Seventh Litany. Stella Matutina.
content:
혻혻혻혻i.

혻혻혻혻Arise, fair Phoebus! and with looks serene
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Survey the world which late the orb占폻 Queen
혻혻혻혻Did pave with pearl to please enamour'd swains.
혻혻혻혻Arise! Arise! The Dark is bound in chains,
혻혻혻혻And thou'rt immortal, and thy throne is here
혻혻혻혻To sway the seasons, and to make it clear
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻How much we need thee, O thou silent god!
혻혻혻혻That art the crown'd controller of the year.


혻혻혻혻ii.

혻혻혻혻And while the breezes re-construct for thee
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The shimmering clouds; and while, from lea to lea,
혻혻혻혻The great earth reddens with a maid's delight,
혻혻혻혻Behold! I bring to thee, as yesternight,
혻혻혻혻My subject song. Do thou protect apace
혻혻혻혻My peerless one, my Peri with the face
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That is a marvel to the minds of men,
혻혻혻혻And like a flower for humbleness of grace.


혻혻혻혻iii.

혻혻혻혻The earth which loves thee, or I much have err'd,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The glad, green earth which waits, as for a word,
혻혻혻혻The sound of thee, up-shuddering through the morn,
혻혻혻혻The restive earth is pleased when Day is born,
혻혻혻혻And soon will take each separate silent beam
혻혻혻혻As proof of sex,--exulting in the dream
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Of joys to come, and quicken'd and convuls'd,
혻혻혻혻Year after year, by love's triumphant theme.


혻혻혻혻iv.

혻혻혻혻A thousand times the flowers in all the fields
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Will bow to thee; and with their little shields
혻혻혻혻The daisy-folk will muster on the plain.
혻혻혻혻A thousand songs the birds will sing again,
혻혻혻혻As sweet to hear as quiverings of a lute;
혻혻혻혻And she I love will sing, for thy repute,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Full many a song. She sings when she but speaks;
혻혻혻혻And when she's near the birds should all be mute.


혻혻혻혻v.

혻혻혻혻O my Belov占폻! from thy curtain'd bed
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Arise, rejoice, uplift thy golden head,
혻혻혻혻And be an instant, while I muse on this,
혻혻혻혻As nude as statues, and as good to kiss
혻혻혻혻As dear St. Agnes when she met her death,
혻혻혻혻Unclad and pure and patient of her breath,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And with the grace of God for wedding-gown,
혻혻혻혻As many an ancient story witnesseth.


혻혻혻혻vi.

혻혻혻혻The bath, the plunge, the combing of the hair,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻All this I view,--a sight beyond compare
혻혻혻혻Since Daphne died in all the varied charms
혻혻혻혻Of her chaste body,--rounded regal arms,
혻혻혻혻And shape supreme, too fair for human gaze,
혻혻혻혻But not too fair to win the mirror's praise
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That throbs to see thee in thy d占퐏habille
혻혻혻혻And loves thee well through all the nights and days.


혻혻혻혻vii.

혻혻혻혻I see thee thus in fancy, as in books
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A man may see the na占폸ds of the brooks;--
혻혻혻혻As one entranced by potions aptly given
혻혻혻혻May see the angels where they walk in Heaven,
혻혻혻혻And may not greet them in their high estate.
혻혻혻혻For who shall guess the riddle wrought of Fate
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Till he be dead? And who that lives a span
혻혻혻혻Shall thwart the Future where it lies in wait?


혻혻혻혻viii.

혻혻혻혻And now to-day a word I dare not write
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Starts to my lips, as when a baffled knight
혻혻혻혻Witholds a song which fain he would repeat;
혻혻혻혻For lo! the sense thereof is passing sweet.
혻혻혻혻And, like a cup that's full, my heart is fill'd
혻혻혻혻With new desires and quiverings new-distill'd
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻From old delights; and all my pulses throb
혻혻혻혻As at the touch of dreams divinely-will'd.


혻혻혻혻ix.

혻혻혻혻Who talks of comfort when he sees thee not
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And feels no fragrance of the happy lot
혻혻혻혻Which violets feel, when call'd upon to lie
혻혻혻혻On thy white breast? And who with amorous eye
혻혻혻혻Looks at the dear tomb of the shuddering flowers,
혻혻혻혻The two-fold tomb where daintily for hours
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻They droop and muse,--who looks, I say, at these
혻혻혻혻And will not own the witchery of thy powers?


혻혻혻혻x.

혻혻혻혻Who speaks of glory and the force of love,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And thou not near, my maiden-minded dove!
혻혻혻혻With all the coyness, all the beauty-sheen,
혻혻혻혻Of thy rapt face? A fearless virgin-queen,--
혻혻혻혻A queen of peace art thou,--and on thy head
혻혻혻혻The golden light of all thy hair is shed
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Most nimbus-like and most suggestive, too,
혻혻혻혻Of youthful saints enshrined and garlanded.


혻혻혻혻xi.

혻혻혻혻Thou'rt Nature's own; and when a word of thine
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Rings on the air, and when the Voice Divine
혻혻혻혻We call the lark upfloats amid the blue,
혻혻혻혻I know not which is which, for both are true,
혻혻혻혻Both meant for Heaven, though foster'd here below.
혻혻혻혻And when the silences around me flow,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I think of lilies and the face of thee
혻혻혻혻Which hath compell'd my manhood's overthrow.


혻혻혻혻xii.

혻혻혻혻O blue-eyed Rapture with the radiant locks!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻O thou for whom, athwart the fever-shocks
혻혻혻혻Of life and death and misery and much sin,
혻혻혻혻I'd sell salvation! There's a prize to win
혻혻혻혻And thou'rt its voucher; there's a wonder-prize,
혻혻혻혻Unknown till now beneath the vaulted skies,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And thou'rt its symbol; thou'rt its essence fair,
혻혻혻혻Its full completion form'd adoring-wise!


혻혻혻혻xiii.

혻혻혻혻Yes, I will tell thee how I love thee best,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And all my thoughts of thee shall be confess'd
혻혻혻혻And none withheld, not e'en the witless one
혻혻혻혻Which late I harbor'd when the mounting sun
혻혻혻혻Burst from a cloud,--the moon a mile away,
혻혻혻혻As if in hiding from the lord of day,--
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻As if, at times, the moon were like thyself,
혻혻혻혻And fear'd the semblance of a master's sway.


혻혻혻혻xiv.

혻혻혻혻I love thee dearly when thine eyes are dim
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With unshed tears; for then they seem to swim
혻혻혻혻In liquid blessedness, and unto me
혻혻혻혻There comes the memory of a god's decree
혻혻혻혻Which said of old:--"Be all men evermore,
혻혻혻혻All men and maids whose hearts are passion-sore,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Acclaim'd in Heaven!" and all day long I muse
혻혻혻혻On hope's divine and deathless prophet-lore.


혻혻혻혻xv.

혻혻혻혻I love thee when the soft endearing flush
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Invades thy face, and dimples in the blush
혻혻혻혻Bespeak attention,--as a rose's pout
혻혻혻혻Absorbs the stillness when the sun is out,
혻혻혻혻And all the air retains the glow thereof.
혻혻혻혻In all the world there is not light enough
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Nor sheen enough, all day, nor any warmth,
혻혻혻혻Till thou be near me, arm'd with some rebuff!


혻혻혻혻xvi.

혻혻혻혻And how I love thee when thy startled eyes
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Look out at me, enrapt in that surprise
혻혻혻혻Which marks an epoch in the life I lead,--
혻혻혻혻As if they guess'd the scope of Eros' creed
혻혻혻혻And all the mirth and malice of his wiles.
혻혻혻혻For it is wondrous when my Lady smiles,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And all the ground is holy where she treads,
혻혻혻혻And all the air is thrill'd for many miles!


혻혻혻혻xvii.

혻혻혻혻In every mood of thine thou art my joy,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And, day by day, to shield thee from annoy,
혻혻혻혻I'd do the deeds that slaves were bound unto
혻혻혻혻With stabs for payment,--shuddering through and through
혻혻혻혻With their much labour; and I'd deem it grand
혻혻혻혻To die for thee if, after touch of hand,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I might but kiss thee as a lover doth;
혻혻혻혻For I should then be king of all the land.


혻혻혻혻xviii.

혻혻혻혻But Father Time, old Time with Janus-face
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Looks o'er the sphere, and sees no fitting place
혻혻혻혻For thine acceptance; for the thrones of earth
혻혻혻혻Are much too mean, and in thy maiden worth
혻혻혻혻Thou'rt crown'd enough, and throned in very sooth
혻혻혻혻More than the queens who lord it in their youth
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻O'er men's convictions; and He names thy name
혻혻혻혻As one belov'd of Nature and of Truth.


혻혻혻혻xix.

혻혻혻혻He sees the nights, he sees the veering days,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The sweet spring season with its hymn of praise,
혻혻혻혻The summer, frondage-proud, the autumn pale,
혻혻혻혻The winter worn with withering of the gale,--
혻혻혻혻All this he sees; and now, to-day, in June,
혻혻혻혻He, too, recalls that rapturous afternoon
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻When all the fields and flowers were like a dream,
혻혻혻혻And all the winds the offshoot of a tune.


혻혻혻혻xx.

혻혻혻혻So I will cease to clamour for the past,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And seek suspension of my doubts at last,
혻혻혻혻In some new way till Fate becomes my friend.
혻혻혻혻I will re-gain the right to re-defend
혻혻혻혻The love I bear to thee, for good or ill.
혻혻혻혻For though, 'tis said, our griefs have power to kill,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Mine let me live, in mine unworthiness,
혻혻혻혻That, spurn'd of thee, my lips may praise thee still!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Eric Mackay represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Eric Mackay's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
